steps.title: State the condition for infinitely many solutions
steps.content: An equation in $x$ has infinitely many solutions when both sides are identical expressions for all $x$. Factor the left side: $$5x + 20 = 5(x + 4)$$ So the equation becomes: $$5(x+4) = \frac{a(x+4)}{2}$$

steps.title: Solve for a
steps.content: Multiply both sides by $2$: $$10(x+4)=a(x+4)$$ For this to hold for every $x$, the coefficient of $(x+4)$ must match, so $a=10$. A direct calculation using two simple $x$-values also returns $a=10$.

steps.title: Test all four choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — $0$: $a=0$ gives $5x+20=0$, true only when $x=-4$ — exactly one solution, not infinitely many. ✗
  • Choice B — $4$: $a=4$ gives $5x+20 = 2x + 8$. Coefficients $5 \neq 2$, so exactly one solution. ✗
  • Choice C — $10$: $a=10$ gives right side $\frac{10(x+4)}{2} = 5(x+4) = 5x+20$. Both sides identical — infinitely many solutions. ✓
  • Choice D — $20$: $a=20$ gives right side $\frac{20(x+4)}{2} = 10x+40$. Coefficients $5 \neq 10$, so exactly one solution. ✗
This matches — C is correct.
